title: Nocturne in Eb Major, Op. 9 No. 2

musicalLanguage.description: This transcription of Chopin's Nocturne in Eb Major runs 123 measures at 120 BPM in 4/4 time with 1,299 notes, and the stated Eb major is strongly confirmed (statedKeyFit 0.894; the content detects D# major, the same pitches spelled with sharps instead of flats, at a 0.258 margin). The left hand carries a waltz-like accompaniment figure that recurs four separate times without transposition — at measures 5-14, 18-28, 45-55, and 72-83 — while the right hand grows steadily more ornamented above it. The chord windows spell out Eb major's own diatonic family under its sharp-spelled names: the tonic D# ties as the most-cited label (15 windows, 12% of measures considered) with G# (the IV) and D#m7 close behind, alongside implied touches of Cm and A#7. The piece's real drama arrives at measure 112: 28 onsets, every one of them in the right hand alone, while the left hand rests through a five-measure silence (measures 110-114) — a written-out free cadenza before the piece settles into near-total silence at its final two measures.
musicalLanguage.structure: Twelve structural regions from the section-lens pass, the two sharpest boundaries opening at measure 62 (novelty 7.558) and measure 116 (6.092) — the approach to the cadenza and the final closing gesture. The same 31-note left-hand waltz figure anchors four regions of the piece (measures 5-14, 18-28, 45-55, 72-83, strong pattern evidence, compression ratio 3.543), and an even larger 88-note left-hand span covers nearly half the piece across just two occurrences (measures 20-47 and 47-75). Above that steady foundation, the right hand's ornamentation keeps changing — the piece never repeats the same melodic figure as literally as the bass repeats its waltz pattern, which is exactly how progressive variation over a fixed accompaniment works. The free cadenza at measure 112 (28 onsets, right hand only) sits inside the longest left-hand silence in the piece (measures 110-114, 5 measures), and the piece closes with two fully silent measures (122-123) after the sparsest passage in its final ten bars.
keyMoments: The left-hand waltz figure at measures 5-14 restates without transposition three more times — at measures 18-28, 45-55, and 72-83 — which teaches the core nocturne technique: a fixed, steady accompaniment frees the right hand to grow more ornamented with every return, rather than the harmony itself changing.

Measure 112's 28 onsets, every single one in the right hand while the left hand sits silent, demonstrates a written-out free cadenza — the analysis's densest measure in the entire piece is also its most soloistic, a skill that separates florid rubato playing from strict-tempo playing.

The chord windows confirm Eb major's own diatonic family under the tool's sharp spelling — D# (the tonic) as the single most-cited label, G# (the IV) not far behind, and A#7 (the dominant seventh) appearing near the cadenza — showing tonic-subdominant-dominant motion holding the piece together even through heavy ornamentation.

The piece closes on total silence at measures 122-123, the endpoint of a section that opens with the sharpest novelty boundary outside the cadenza (measure 116, novelty 6.092) — a model for how a nocturne can end by simply stopping rather than through a loud final cadence.
teachingGoals: Learn the 31-note left-hand waltz figure once (measures 5-14) and practice recognizing its three later returns (18-28, 45-55, 72-83) as the same shape wearing different right-hand ornamentation.
Practice the measure 112 cadenza as its own unit, completely free of the left hand's steady pulse — build the freedom to stretch time when no accompaniment is anchoring the beat.

Recognize the implied D#-G#-A#7 harmonic family (the tonic, subdominant, and dominant seventh under sharp spelling) by ear, and listen for how it keeps returning under increasingly ornamented melodic lines.

Develop control over the transition into total silence at measures 122-123 — practice letting a phrase fully decay rather than cutting it off, since the piece's ending is an absence of sound, not a final chord.
styleTips: Keep the left-hand waltz figure absolutely steady through all four of its appearances (measures 5-14, 18-28, 45-55, 72-83) — listen for it staying identical in rhythm even as the right hand above it grows more ornamented each time.
Use rubato freely in the right hand, especially through the measure 112 cadenza where the left hand rests entirely — listen for the phrase breathing like a singer taking a free, unmeasured breath.

Pedal generously through the ornamented returns, but lift and change with every new harmony implied by the D#-G#-A#7 family — listen for the bass notes staying clear under the florid right hand rather than blurring together.
Let the final two silent measures (122-123) actually happen — don't fill them with sound or rush to a bow; listen for the room going quiet after the sparsest closing bars.
